text: We're demonstrating cervical tracheal resection for a patient who has a tracheostomy stoma stricture.
The patient presented with stridor and was found to have a tight triangulated stricture in the cervical trachea immediately beneath the
tracheostomy, a stoma scar.
So for this approach, the patient's supine with the shoulders elevated, the neck extended.
You'll see a collar incision made in the lower aspect of the neck, overlying the tracheal stricture.
The first step of the procedure is
dividing the platysma muscle to gain adequate exposure to the subplatysmal layer.
And this is done with standard cautery.
You can see the platysmal fibers being divided here.
A small superficial vein is ligated and divided.
within that subplatysmal layer.
So you have a good appreciation of the exposure
It's a relatively short collar incision, very well tolerated by the patient, provides superb access to the lesion.
And with upward elevation, the caudal extent of the subplatysmal dissection is conducted.
And we like to extend this subplatysmal dissection to the lateral edges of the wound.
So we have good access for subsequent retraction.
The markers which we pay particular attention to, obviously the sternomastoid muscles, which you can appreciate.
And as the flap is raised proximally, you can appreciate the strap muscles lying on either side of the midline.
This proximal dissection should be conducted up to the level of the superior aspect of the thyroid cartilage.
This provides increased mobilization of the proximal airway to avoid tension on the subsequent anastomosis.
A similar dissection is conducted caudally, raising the subplatysmal flap inferiorly.
staying immediately superficial to the sternomastoid muscle on either side.
And this dissection is carried down to the level of the sternum and allows us to subsequently expose the sternal notch for an appropriate midline marker.
This just provides increased exposure for subsequent retraction and visualization of the operative field.
For the superficial portion of the dissection, use of standard electrocautery is quite safe and
provides an effective means of maintaining a bloodless field.

With the upward and inferior retraction of the subplatysmal flaps, we can appreciate the strap muscles in the midline, and here we see the strap muscles being divided throughout the entire extent of the dissection.
further aiding in the mobilization of the airway beneath the strap muscles.
Here we see the soft tissues leading into the sternal notch being better identified and allowing adequate inferior exposure for the subsequent mobilization of the trachea.

A second Wheatlander retractor provides excellent lateral exposure and we can now appreciate the strap muscles being divided down into the, to the level of the thoracic inlet at the level of the sternal notch.
Strap muscles are further divided.
Here we can appreciate the division of the thyroid isthmus.
at about the level of the second tracheal ring.
Further lateral mobilization using a second Wielander retractor.
You see cautery being used on the anterolateral aspect of the airway on each side.
For more posterior dissection we use sharp dissection and avoid the use of standard cautery preferring
a bipolar cautery arrangement to avoid any possibility of injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerves.

Here you can see the pre-tracheofascia incised and blunt dissection of the anterior trachea conducted well down into the mediastinum to try and eliminate any possibility of tension on the subsequent anastomosis.
with further lateral retraction and a stay suture placed at the site of the tracheal deformity, which is readily appreciated.
This allows easy lateral mobilization of the trachea with the stay suture pulled to one side and subsequently to the other.
It's critical to maintain the
dissection on the surface of the airway.
Here we see the laryngeal cartilage, the cricoid cartilage beneath that, and the tracheal deformity three or four rings below the cricoid.
The airway is then opened immediately above the deformity.

and we make an anterior incision which is then extended on either lateral aspect of the trachea to expose the tracheal lumen and the stomal stricture on the left side inferior aspect of the cervical trachea.
You can appreciate the endotracheal tube lying across the stricture.
We then utilize a series of stay sutures to help with retraction and display the defect in the airway.
And these traction sutures also provide correct orientation for the subsequent anastomosis.
We place a stay suture on the cartilaginous membranous corner on each side to allow adequate
retraction and clear visualization of the airway for subsequent posterior membranous tracheal division.
Here we see the endotracheal tube cuff deflated being withdrawn into the proximal trachea so that it is up and out of the operative field.
Subsequently, we will utilize a cross-field strategy.
You can appreciate the triangular deformity in the airway.
Typically, the posterior wall of the airway is relatively preserved in these stomal strictures.
Such strictures are generally quite short.
Here in this particular case, the stricture is no more than two tracheal rings or approximately one centimeter in length.
and the distal tracheal wall and airway caliber are essentially normal.
We now place the similar series of stay sutures for orientation and retraction, one on the anterior tracheal wall and one on each posterior cartilaginous membranous junction.
You can appreciate the cross-field ventilation strategy which maintains adequate oxygenation and ventilation for the patient and really does not get in the way of the operative field.
And much of the operation, the resection of the stricture and the subsequent anastomosis can be conducted with that tube in place.
You see us resecting the tracheal stricture from the field here, maintaining very close proximity to the airway wall as we conduct that dissection, thereby protecting both recurrent laryngeal nerves.
We don't make any effort to identify the nerves.
But by staying on the airway wall and utilizing a bipolar cautery strategy, which you can appreciate, it avoids injury to either right or left recurrent laryngeal nerve.
And this demonstrates division of the posterior membranous airway on the superior aspect of the stricture.
to complete the resection of the tracheostomy stomal stricture.
And once resected, we can get a better
appreciation of the typical triangular shape of these strictures where the anterior wall is deformed by the previously placed anterior tracheostomy wound and subsequent fibrous healing.
Quite typically, the posterior tracheal wall is actually preserved.
The triangular shape is achieved by the anterior and lateral collapse of the cartilaginous airway, and you can appreciate that
external deformity as well.
Once again, a short segment of airway, approximately a centimeter in length or two or three tracheal rings.
Prior to beginning the anastomosis of the proximal and distal trachea, stay sutures are placed to maintain correct orientation
and alignment of the anastomosis as it is created.
Once again, the cross-field ventilation really provides no obstacle to careful exposure and precise alignment during re-approximation of the airway.
The posterior wall of the trachea, the membranous airway, is approximated first
And we utilize a continuous suture of 4-0 PDS or monofilament suture, which is placed in running fashion from one side of the posterior wall to the other.
And this achieves nice mucosal apposition.
and maintenance of the normal airway configuration.

You'll see that there really is very little tension on this posterior aspect of the anastomosis because we've managed a very good proximal and distal mobilization of the airway to eliminate any possibility of undue tension.
Typically we utilize a corner suture in the membranous cartilaginous junction.
My preference is to utilize 4-0 or 3-0 Vicryl for this purpose.
These corner sutures are tied and then secured to the running posterior membranous suture.
And this completes the posterior
reconstruction of the cervical trachea.
When these corner sutures are tied in place there really is no longer any need for the previously placed stay sutures and they can be removed and discarded.
These corner sutures also provide a very nice retraction strategy to bring the trachea up out of the wound
for precise placement of the anterior cartilaginous vicral sutures.
Once again, this can all be accomplished with the cross-field ventilation tubing in place.

The anterior cartilaginous airway is then re-approximated using a series of interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures placed several millimeters apart, each suture incorporating one tracheal ring above and below the anastomosis.
You typically begin on the posterior corner and come out towards the midline with these series of sutures.
And prior to tying these sutures, we will plan to remove the cross-field ventilation endotracheal tube.
and advance the oral endotracheal tube placed at the initial phase of the operation.
You'll see that shortly.
In addition, prior to tying these sutures, it's important to eliminate the neck extension.
So a portable inflatable bag placed beneath the patient's shoulders will be deflated
so as to reduce neck extension prior to tying these sutures, once again in an effort to reduce any element of tension on the anastomosis.
You can see that we do make an effort to incorporate one tracheal ring above and below the anastomosis with each suture.
As the cross-field ventilation tubing is removed, it gives us an opportunity to aspirate any blood clot which may have accumulated in the airway above the tracheal ring.
cuff above the endotracheal cuff.
As shown here is the advancement of the oral endotracheal tube across the anastomosis so that the balloon of the endotracheal tube is in the distal airway.
And with the balloon inflated, we once again have a secure airway and can approximate the anterior cartilaginous sutures without any
tension on the level at the anastomosis.
As each suture is tied the one beside it is placed on traction once again to reduce any element of tension.
It also I think provides
better alignment of the airway with good end-to-end apposition.
We generally don't try and create any aspect of intussusception, but rather aim for good end-to-end approximation.
So here we have the anastomosis lying in the soft tissues of the neck.
We make a special point to close the soft tissues of the neck
providing some fixation to the anterior aspect of the anastomosis.
Now, prior to completing this step of the operation, the anastomosis is tested under saline with the endotracheal tube cuff deflated to confirm that there's no leak.
However,
In a situation like this where there's really no tension on the anastomosis and excellent visualization with the reconstruction, there's very little chance of an air leak from the anastomosis.
The thyroid isthmus is re-approximated across the airway.
You can appreciate we take a bite of the anterior airway wall to secure the soft tissues of the neck to the airway wall, nicely covering the anastomosis.
and securing the airway above and below the anastomosis, once again in an effort to try and eliminate any possibility of tension.

Strap muscles are then brought across and sewn to the front of the cricothyroid membrane and subsequently to the anterior aspect of the thyroid cartilage, thereby completely burying the airway within the soft tissues of the neck.
This is a very standard routine closure of the neck.
The next phase of the closure is a re-approximation of the platysma, which we conduct with a running suture of
3-0 Vicryl.
You can appreciate that the neck extension has been removed.
We see the patient's chin is much closer to the wound and to our working area than it had been on the previous earlier phase of the operation with the neck extended.
You can also appreciate the median sternotomy scar from her prior cardiac surgical procedure.
This patient developed respiratory failure following her cardiac surgery.
This required cervical tracheostomy.

The patient actually did quite well for some months subsequent to that, but then slowly developed symptoms of stridor or inspiratory wheezing, which led to the diagnosis of the tracheostomal stricture.
As we displayed, these stomal strictures have a rather triangular configuration in contrast to

endotracheal cuff strictures, which generally appear a little further distal in the airway because they're produced by the cuff of the endotracheal tube or tracheostomy tube rather than the stoma of a tracheostomy.
In addition, those cuff strictures typically have a circumferential appearance rather than a triangular appearance.
So although their configuration is somewhat different, the approach to their surgical management is very much the same.
These stomal strictures, as we saw in this patient, are very readily approached surgically utilizing this anterior transcervical approach.

Occasionally, a cuff stricture, typically involving the lower aspect of the more inferior aspect of the trachea, can sometimes be approached utilizing the same trans-cervical approach, but occasionally a trans-thoracic approach either by sternotomy or posterolateral thoracotomy is required to gain adequate access for more distal lesions.
And this final step of the closure is a simple subcuticular suture which provides an excellent cosmetic result.

In former times, we utilized a skin suture or skin staple technique, but this is subcuticular closure with the Dermabond dressing is a much better tolerated closure for the patient, much easier to manage.
Now when we finish with the wound closure and the application of the Dermabond, we will remove the surgical drapes, which allows us careful exposure of the anterior neck and anterior chest.
for correct placement of a guardian stitch which is placed immediately posterior to the mandible on its upper aspect and on its inferior aspect to the sterno-maneuver junction or the angle of Louis.
which we're palpating here.
And a deep cutaneous and subcutaneous bite provides a secure placement of the suture, which is then tied in relatively loose fashion with the neck partially flexed.
And the purpose of this is to maintain that
correct and relatively neutral position of the neck.
You'll see that the patient's neck is not excessively flexed.
But this suture is a reminder of the patient to maintain that neutral neck position and avoid any neck extension and the resultant tension in the anastomosis.
The suture is left in place for approximately one week until we've had a chance to bronchoscope the patient and confirm adequate healing.
